1: Joseph threw himself on his father and wept over him and kissed him — Joseph’s grief at Jacob’s death: the son who weeps over the father. The deep filial grief of Joseph is the type of Christ’s grief over those he loves. “Jesus wept” (John 11:35) — the shortest verse in Scripture, at the tomb of Lazarus. Christ also weeps over Jerusalem: “As he approached Jerusalem and saw the city, he wept over it” (Luke 19:41) — weeping over those who would not receive him, as a son weeps over a father. The God who grieves — “in all their distress he too was distressed” (Isaiah 63:9) — is not remote from human loss. The weeping son who throws himself on his father and kisses him is the type of the incarnate Son whose grief is real and whose love is expressed in the physical gesture of presence: not distant sympathy but embodied sorrow.
2: Then Joseph directed the physicians in his service to embalm his father Israel. So the physicians embalmed him — the embalming of Jacob: the preservation of the body for the journey to Canaan. The deliberate preservation of the patriarch’s body — the care taken so that it may complete its journey to the Promised Land — is the type of the resurrection hope that values the physical body. The NT’s insistence on the bodily resurrection: “the body that is sown is perishable, it is raised imperishable… it is sown in dishonor, it is raised in glory” (1 Corinthians 15:42–43). The body that is embalmed for the journey — preserved for the destination — is the type of the body that is “sown in weakness” but “raised in power.” The physical body matters; Joseph’s care for his father’s body is the type of the care that honors what God will raise.
3: taking a full forty days, for that was the time required for embalming. And the Egyptians mourned for him seventy days — forty days of preparation; seventy days of national mourning. The forty days recur throughout the Bible’s decisive moments: forty years of wilderness wandering; forty days of Jesus’ temptation (Matthew 4:2); forty days of resurrection appearances (Acts 1:3). The forty that marks a period of preparation and testing. The seventy days of Egyptian mourning for the covenant patriarch is the nations’ mourning for Israel’s father — the type of the nations’ recognition of the covenant: “Blessed is the nation whose God is the LORD, the people he chose for his inheritance” (Psalms 33:12). The Egyptians who mourn for Jacob are honoring one who belonged to a different covenant — the type of the world that will recognize the covenant in the last days.
4: When the days of mourning had passed, Joseph said to Pharaoh’s household, “If I have found favor in your eyes, speak to Pharaoh for me” — Joseph’s diplomatic approach through Pharaoh’s household: he does not approach Pharaoh directly (he is in mourning and perhaps ritually unclean), but asks intermediaries to speak on his behalf. The mediator who uses intermediaries — who works through the structures of the society he inhabits to accomplish covenant purposes — is the type of Christ’s use of human channels for divine purposes. Paul: “We are therefore Christ’s ambassadors, as though God were making his appeal through us” (2 Corinthians 5:20). The one who has the greatest authority working through proper channels — not forcing his will but asking through appropriate means — is the pattern of God’s incarnational approach to human history.
5: “My father made me swear an oath and said, ‘I am about to die; bury me in the tomb I dug for myself in the land of Canaan.’ Now let me go up and bury my father; then I will return” — Joseph’s appeal to the binding nature of the oath sworn to his father and his promise to return to Egypt. The covenant oath that binds even Pharaoh’s permission to honor it. The binding nature of the oath sworn to the father: God’s faithfulness to his covenant oaths with the patriarchs (Hebrews 6:17–18: “Because God wanted to make the unchanging nature of his purpose very clear to the heirs of what was promised, he confirmed it with an oath. God did this so that… we who have fled to take hold of the hope set before us may be greatly encouraged”). The oath sworn to the father is the ground of confident action; the divine oath to Abraham is the ground of confident faith.
6: Pharaoh said, “Go up and bury your father, as he made you swear to do” — Pharaoh’s permission: “as he made you swear to do.” The highest earthly authority bows to the covenant oath sworn before God. The kings of the earth who recognize and submit to the covenant obligations — even when they are not party to the covenant — are acting according to the divine ordering of all human authority. Paul: “Let everyone be subject to the governing authorities, for there is no authority except that which God has established” (Romans 13:1). Pharaoh’s honoring of the patriarchal oath is the type of the earthly authority that serves covenant purposes: “kings will be your foster fathers, and their queens your nursing mothers” (Isaiah 49:23). God uses even the highest pagan authority to accomplish covenant goals.
7: So Joseph went up to bury his father. All Pharaoh’s officials accompanied him — the dignitaries of his court and all the dignitaries of Egypt — the great funeral procession: all Pharaoh’s officials, all the dignitaries of Egypt. The covenant patriarch buried with the honor of the greatest empire of the day. The world’s power attending the burial of the man of faith — “He was considered of great value in the eyes of their officials” (Psalms 105:20 re. Joseph; the honor flows to Jacob). The nations attending the burial of the covenant patriarch is the type of the nations ultimately recognizing the covenant: “All the nations you have made will come and worship before you, Lord; they will bring glory to your name” (Psalms 86:9). The procession of Egypt’s greatest to bury Jacob is the type of the eschatological procession of the nations to the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:24).
8: together with all the members of Joseph’s household and his brothers and those belonging to his father’s household. Only their children and their flocks and herds were left in Goshen — the comprehensive assembly for the burial journey: Joseph’s household, the brothers, the father’s household, Egyptian officials — all together. The covenant family and the nations traveling together to honor the patriarch in the Promised Land is the type of the eschatological community: “There is neither Jew nor Gentile, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus” (Galatians 3:28). The mixed procession — covenant family and Egyptian dignitaries — anticipates the mixed community of the new covenant, where Jew and Gentile travel together to the promised destination.
9: Chariots and horsemen also went up with him. It was a very large company — the military escort: chariots and horsemen. The full military honor for the patriarch’s burial. The armies of Egypt accompanying the one family that holds the covenant promises is the type of the angelic escort that accompanies the covenant community: “The chariots of God are tens of thousands and thousands of thousands; the Lord has come from Sinai into his sanctuary” (Psalms 68:17). Elisha’s vision: “those who are with us are more than those who are with them” — “the hills full of horses and chariots of fire” (2 Kings 6:16–17). The visible military honor of Egypt is the type of the invisible divine protection that accompanies the covenant family.
10: When they reached the threshing floor of Atad, near the Jordan, they lamented loudly and bitterly; and there Joseph observed a seven-day period of mourning for his father — the mourning at the threshing floor of Atad — the threshing floor where Jacob is lamented before the Jordan. The threshing floor is the biblical place of judgment and separation (the chaff from the grain). The mourning at the threshing floor before the crossing of the Jordan is the type of the sorrow and testing that precedes the entry into the promise. John the Baptist at the Jordan: “his winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor, gathering his wheat into the barn and burning up the chaff with unquenchable fire” (Matthew 3:12). The seven-day mourning period — a full week of grief — mirrors the completeness of the mourning before the resurrection dawn: the disciples’ full grief of the burial period before the resurrection morning.
11: When the Canaanites who lived there saw the mourning at the threshing floor of Atad, they said, “The Egyptians are holding a solemn ceremony of mourning.” That is why that place near the Jordan is called Abel Mizraim — the nations who witness the covenant family’s grief name the place “mourning of Egypt” (Abel Mizraim). The world that observes the covenant community’s mourning and names the place after what it sees. The church’s testimony in grief: “we do not grieve like the rest of mankind, who have no hope” (1 Thessalonians 4:13) — but the grief is real, and the world notices. The Canaanites who see the great mourning of Egypt at the Jordan are the type of the watching world that observes the church’s life in all its dimensions, including grief. The name given by the nations — “Abel Mizraim” — marks the place forever with the covenant family’s sorrow.
12: So Jacob’s sons did as he had commanded them — the obedient fulfillment of the patriarch’s command by the twelve sons: they did exactly what Jacob had instructed. The obedience of the twelve sons to the dying patriarch is the type of the disciples’ obedience to Christ’s commission: “They went out and preached everywhere, and the Lord worked with them” (Mark 16:20). The twelve who were commissioned by the dying patriarch carry out the mission without deviation. The covenant community’s faithfulness to the commission — doing what the one who sent them commanded — is the type of apostolic faithfulness: “I received from the Lord what I also passed on to you” (1 Corinthians 11:23).
13: They carried him to the land of Canaan and buried him in the cave in the field of Machpelah, near Mamre, which Abraham had bought along with the field as a burial place from Ephron the Hittite — the mission accomplished: Jacob buried in the purchased cave in the Promised Land. The son who carries the father to the place of promise and buries him there — the mission completed exactly as commanded. The type of Christ’s faithful completion of the Father’s mission: “Father, I have revealed you to those whom you gave me out of the world… I protected them and kept them safe” (John 17:6, 12). The twelve sons who carry Jacob to Machpelah are the type of the apostolic mission that carries the covenant testimony to its promised destination: “you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth” (Acts 1:8).
14: After burying his father, Joseph returned to Egypt, together with his brothers and all the others who had gone with him to bury his father — the return to Egypt after the burial — the mission completed, now returning to the place of service. Joseph returns to Egypt — to the station God has given him — after honoring his father. The type of Christ’s return to the Father after completing his earthly mission: “I came from the Father and entered the world; now I am leaving the world and going back to the Father” (John 16:28). The ascension does not end the work but returns the Son to the Father from whom the mission came and to whom the completed work is presented: “I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do” (John 17:4).
15: When Joseph’s brothers saw that their father was dead, they said, “What if Joseph holds a grudge against us and pays us back for all the wrongs we did to him?” — the brothers’ fear after Jacob’s death: the protector is gone; Joseph might now exercise the vengeance he has been restraining. The fear of divine retribution after the removal of the “covering” is the type of the guilty conscience’s approach to God: “it is a dreadful thing to fall into the hands of the living God” (Hebrews 10:31). The brothers who have lived with the knowledge of their guilt for twenty-three years (Genesis 37 to Genesis 50) and who fear the delayed judgment are the type of those who live under the awareness of guilt and the dread of God’s justice. Joseph’s answer (v19–21) is the gospel’s answer to this fear.
16: So they sent word to Joseph, saying, “Your father left these instructions before he died” — the brothers send word rather than going directly — they are afraid to face Joseph in person. The prayer that is offered through intermediaries — not daring to come directly — is the type of the prayer of the guilty conscience that is afraid to come directly to God. The brothers need the father’s name to give them courage: “your father left these instructions.” The mediation of the father’s name — praying in the father’s name — is the type of prayer in Jesus’ name: “I will do whatever you ask in my name” (John 14:13). The brothers approach through the father’s authority; the believer approaches through the Son’s name.
17: “Say to Joseph: I ask you to forgive your brothers the sins and the wrongs they committed in treating you so badly.” Now please forgive the sins of the servants of the God of your father — the request for forgiveness in the father’s name and the name of the God of the patriarchs. The prayer of forgiveness: “forgive your brothers the sins and the wrongs they committed in treating you so badly.” This is the explicit request for the forgiveness of real sin — “sins and wrongs” — done against a real person. The gospel’s forgiveness is equally concrete: the real sins and wrongs done against God, forgiven not by ignoring them but by bearing their cost. “When they said this, Joseph wept” — the plea for forgiveness moves the one to whom it is addressed to tears before words. The tears that precede the word of forgiveness are the type of God’s own grief over human sin: “in all their distress he too was distressed” (Isaiah 63:9).
18: His brothers then came and threw themselves down before him. “We are your slaves,” they said — the final and complete prostration of the brothers before Joseph: “We are your slaves.” The fulfillment of Genesis 37’s dreams is now total — not the polite bow of subjects but the complete self-surrender of those who acknowledge their guilt and submit entirely. “Jesus Christ is Lord” (Philippians 2:11) — the universal acknowledgment of Christ’s authority that the brothers’ prostration prefigures. The genuinely surrendered life: “I have been crucified with Christ and I no longer live, but Christ lives in me” (Galatians 2:20). The brothers who threw themselves down before Joseph are the type of the believer who comes to Christ not as a co-equal petitioner but as one who surrenders: “I am no longer worthy to be called your son; make me like one of your hired servants” (Luke 15:19). The total surrender that produces the total grace of v19–21.
19: But Joseph said to them, “Don’t be afraid. Am I in the place of God?” — Joseph’s response: the refusal of revenge and the question “Am I in the place of God?” The one who has every human right to exact vengeance refuses to claim the place of ultimate judge. The type of Christ’s humility: “I did not come to judge the world, but to save the world” (John 12:47); “there is no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus” (Romans 8:1). “Am I in the place of God?” — the question points beyond itself to the one who IS the presence of God incarnate (“The Father and I are one,” John 10:30) and who nevertheless came not to execute judgment but to absorb it. The savior-figure who declines the role of judge in order to be the Savior is one of the OT’s clearest anticipations of the gospel’s logic: the Judge who becomes the atoning sacrifice.
20: “You intended to harm me, but God intended it for good to accomplish what is now being done — the saving of many lives” — the theological key of the entire Joseph narrative and the OT’s clearest statement of the gospel’s logic: the same event — the brothers’ sale of Joseph — was simultaneously human evil and divine providence; human wickedness and salvific purpose. Acts 2:23: “This man was handed over to you by God’s deliberate plan and foreknowledge; and you, with the help of wicked men, put him to death by nailing him to the cross.” The cross is the supreme fulfillment of Genesis 50:20: the worst human action (the murder of the Son of God) is the means by which God saves the many. Romans 8:28: “in all things God works for the good of those who love him.” “The saving of many lives” — the scope of the salvation accomplished through the one man’s suffering: “one righteous act resulted in justification and life for all people” (Romans 5:18). The whole of biblical theology is compressed into this verse.
21: “So then, don’t be afraid. I will provide for you and your children.” And he reassured them and spoke kindly to them — the grace-word that follows the theological statement: provision, reassurance, kind speech. “He spoke kindly to them” — dibber al-libbam — “he spoke to their heart,” the language of intimate comfort and wooing (Hosea 2:14: “I will lead her into the wilderness and speak tenderly to her”). The one who has every right to condemn speaks to the heart of the guilty. The type of the gospel’s grace-word: “Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest” (Matthew 11:28); “God so loved the world” (John 3:16); “there is now no condemnation” (Romans 8:1). Joseph’s kind word to the brothers who sold him is the OT’s most direct narrative type of the gospel’s word of grace to the guilty: provision, peace, and the spoken comfort that reaches the heart.
22: Joseph stayed in Egypt, along with all his father’s family. He lived a hundred and ten years — Joseph’s long life in Egypt with his father’s family — the continuation of the covenant community in the place of sojourning. The covenant family that lives in Egypt — in the world, but sustained by the one who has authority over the whole land — is the type of the church in the world: “My prayer is not that you take them out of the world but that you protect them from the evil one” (John 17:15). Joseph’s hundred and ten years in Egypt — a full and fruitful life in the place of exile — is the type of the flourishing of the covenant community in the world before the final Exodus: “Seek the peace and prosperity of the city to which I have carried you into exile” (Jeremiah 29:7).
23: and saw the third generation of Ephraim’s children. Also the children of Makir son of Manasseh were placed at birth on Joseph’s knees — Joseph lives to see his great-grandchildren — the third and fourth generation. The covenant promise of descendants seen by the patriarch himself: “I am the LORD your God… showing love to a thousand generations of those who love me” (Exodus 20:5–6). The blessing that extends to the third and fourth generation visible within a single lifetime is the earnest of the covenant’s fullness. The children placed on Joseph’s knees — the adoption gesture (as Jacob placed Ephraim and Manasseh on his knees in Genesis 48) — is the type of the new covenant’s adoption: “he predestined us for adoption to sonship through Jesus Christ” (Ephesians 1:5). The generation on the patriarch’s knees is the type of the believer placed into the covenant family through Christ.
24: Then Joseph said to his brothers, “I am about to die. But God will surely come to your aid and take you up out of this land to the land he promised on oath to Abraham, Isaac and Jacob” — Joseph’s dying prophecy: “God will surely come to your aid” (pakod yifkod — a solemn oath formula). This phrase will be the sign of the coming Exodus: when Moses tells the elders “He has surely come to your aid” (Exodus 3:16), they will know the promised deliverance has come (Exodus 4:30–31). Hebrews 11:22: “By faith Joseph, when his end was near, spoke about the exodus of the Israelites from Egypt and gave instructions concerning the burial of his bones.” The dying man who prophesies the coming of God as the moment of deliverance is the type of Christ’s promise of his return: “I will come back and take you to be with me” (John 14:3). “God will surely come” — the OT form of the Parousia promise.
25: And Joseph made the Israelites swear an oath and said, “God will surely come to your aid, and then you must carry my bones up from this place” — the oath about the bones: Joseph’s insistence that his remains participate in the Exodus. His bones will be carried through the Red Sea, through the wilderness, into the Promised Land (Exodus 13:19; Joshua 24:32). Hebrews 11:22 celebrates this as the act of faith: Joseph’s death-instruction about his bones is a declaration of resurrection faith — the body that will enter the Promised Land even if the soul has gone ahead. The type of the Christian’s bodily resurrection hope: “I know that my Redeemer lives, and that in the end he will stand on the earth. And after my skin has been destroyed, yet in my flesh I will see God” (Job 19:25–26). The bones that make the Exodus journey are the OT’s most concrete image of bodily resurrection faith. Every Christian burial is an act of Joseph’s faith: these bones will rise.
26: And Joseph died at the age of a hundred and ten. And after they embalmed him, he was placed in a coffin in Egypt — the final verse of Genesis: the book that began with “In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth” (Genesis 1:1) ends with “a coffin in Egypt.” The book of creation ends with a box in a foreign land. The coffin that waits — not as a permanent ending but as a temporary resting place before the promised Exodus — is the type of every Christian grave: not a final destination but a waiting place. “For as in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive” (1 Corinthians 15:22). The coffin in Egypt holds Joseph until the Exodus; the grave holds the believer until the resurrection. Genesis ends as it must end: with a body in a foreign land, a promise unfulfilled (the Promised Land unoccupied, the Seed of the woman not yet come), and a hope anchored in the word of God: “God will surely come to your aid” (v24). The rest of the canon is the unfolding of that promise.
